rationale_for_baseline_tier: A mild mucocutaneous Behcet's flare with early (not severe) dehydration that is still self-correctable with topical analgesia and oral fluids warrants prompt review and active home management with a tight safety-net — a T2 baseline. It sits close to T3: because the patient lives alone with 12 h of poor intake and postural symptoms, escalate to same-day face-to-face (T3) if the fluid trial fails.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to keep any fluids down, no urine passed, or worsening faintness/near-collapse
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same-day face-to-face assessment for IV fluids
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates dehydration not correctable orally, needing IV rehydration.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you still can't drink after using the numbing gel, stop passing urine, or feel like you might faint, go to urgent care or the emergency department tonight for fluids.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New painful red eye/visual change, genital ulcers, severe headache or neurological symptoms
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests systemic (ocular/neuro) Behcet's needing urgent specialist involvement.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a painful red eye or blurred vision, sores in the genital area, or a severe headache, seek urgent care the same day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one checking in
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for face-to-face review and arrange a friend to check in overnight.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Use the numbing gel before sipping cool water or milk and take small frequent sips — this should let you rehydrate at home. Because you're on your own, ask a friend to check in tonight. If you can't drink at all, stop passing urine, or feel faint, go to urgent care or ED tonight.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Reassess by telehealth after the fluid trial; urgent care/ED tonight if it fails
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Urgent care or ED is appropriate overnight if you cannot maintain fluids.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth review of a mild mucocutaneous Behcet's oral flare with early dehydration; topical lignocaine + corticosteroid prescribed to enable oral rehydration; dehydration and systemic-Behcet's safety-net given; patient lives alone.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as trivial mouth ulcers with self-care only, missing the dehydration risk in someone living alone.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The patient cannot rehydrate, collapses overnight from dehydration with no one present.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: T2 with active management and safety-net is acceptable; the risk is failing to escalate to T3 if the fluid trial fails.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Ambulance/ED dispatch for a mild flare with preserved saliva swallowing, before any analgesia trial, is over-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cannot objectively measure hydration (postural vitals, mucous membranes) remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Use proxy markers (urine output, postural symptoms, saliva swallowing), trial topical analgesia + oral fluids, and set a low threshold for same-day face-to-face review given the patient lives alone.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either dismiss as trivial ulcers or dispatch an ambulance without any assessment or analgesia trial.
